elements.sent-2411-001: Linear Recurrent Neural Networks (LRNNs) such as Mamba, RWKV, GLA, mLSTM, and DeltaNet have emerged as efficient alternatives to Transformers in large language modeling, offering linear scaling with sequence length and improved training efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, LRNNs struggle to perform state-tracking which may impair performance in tasks such as code evaluation or tracking a chess game.
elements.sent-2411-003: Even parity, the simplest state-tracking task, which non-linear RNNs like LSTM handle effectively, cannot be solved by current LRNNs.
elements.sent-2411-004: Recently, \citet{sarrof2024expressive demonstrated that the failure of LRNNs like Mamba to solve parity stems from restricting the value range of their diagonal state-transition matrices to $[0, 1]$ and that incorporating negative values can resolve this issue.}
elements.sent-2411-005: We extend this result to non-diagonal LRNNs, which have recently shown promise in models such as DeltaNet.
elements.sent-2411-006: We prove that finite precision LRNNs with state-transition matrices having only positive eigenvalues cannot solve parity, while complex eigenvalues are needed to count modulo $3$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Notably, we also prove that LRNNs can learn any regular language when their state-transition matrices are products of identity minus vector outer product matrices, each with eigenvalues in the range $[-1, 1]$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our empirical results confirm that extending the eigenvalue range of models like Mamba and DeltaNet to include negative values not only enables them to solve parity but consistently improves their performance on state-tracking tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, pre-training LRNNs with an extended eigenvalue range for language modeling achieves comparable performance and stability while showing promise on code and math data.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our work enhances the expressivity of modern LRNNs, broadening their applicability without changing the cost of training or inference.
elements.sent-2411-011: Transformer architectures~\citep{vaswani_trans have revolutionized NLP but scale quadratically in sequence length, posing computational challenges for long sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-012: To address this, Linear Recurrent Neural Networks (LRNNs) have emerged as promising alternatives that offer linear scaling while maintaining competitive performance~\citep{gu2023mamba, dao2024transformers, yanggated, peng2023rwkv, deletangneural, sun2024learning, poppel2024xlstm .}
elements.sent-2411-013: LRNNs update their state via matrix-vector products with structured and often input-dependent state-transition matrices.
elements.sent-2411-014: The structure of the state-transition matrices largely determines the expressivity of LRNNs.
elements.sent-2411-015: While successful models like Mamba~\citep{gu2023mamba and GLA~\citep{yanggated use diagonal matrices (diagonal LRNN) which only mix tokens along the sequence dimension, recent work explores more complex forms.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Notably, non-diagonal matrices using generalized Householder (GH) transformations, defined as $\mI - \vu\vu^\top$ where $\vu$ is a learnable vector and $\mI$ is the identity, enable models like DeltaNet~\citep{schlag2021learning,yang2024parallelizing and TTT-Linear~\citep{sun2024learning to achieve richer expressiveness through simultaneous token-channel mixing while maintaining efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite these successes, both Transformers and current LRNNs face a fundamental limitation: they struggle to learn how to track the state of even simple finite-state machines from sequences of state-transitions \citep{deletangneural .}
elements.sent-2411-018: This limitation may impair performance on tasks such as entity tracking in narratives, handling nested structures in code, and other reasoning tasks that can benefit from maintaining and updating an internal state over time~\citep{merrillillusion .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Even the simplest state-tracking task, computing the parity of a sequence of bits, cannot be solved by current LRNNs, while non-linear RNNs like LSTM~\citep{hochreiter1997long and sLSTM~\citep{poppel2024xlstm can solve parity~\citep{merrillillusion .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, in contrast to modern linear RNNs, non-linear RNNs lack an efficient method for parallelizing the training across the sequence length.

elements.sent-2411-030: In summary, we make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-031: We prove that any finite precision LRNN with only positive real eigenvalues in the state-transition matrices (most LRNNs used in practice) cannot solve parity at arbitrary sequence lengths (\Cref{thm:parity ), while complex eigenvalues are required to learn counting modulo $3$ (\Cref{thm:modcount ).}}
elements.sent-2411-032: By extending the eigenvalue range, we significantly improve the state-tracking capabilities of LRNNs.
elements.sent-2411-033: We prove that LRNNs with state-transition matrices formed by products of generalized Householder (GH) matrices, each with eigenvalues in the range $[-1,1]$, can learn any regular language (\Cref{thm:regular ), in some cases with just one layer (\Cref{thm:groups ).}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Notably, this range extension allows LRNNs, using just one GH matrix (like DeltaNet), to learn substantially harder tasks, such as the composition of permutations of two elements, compared to diagonal LRNNs.
elements.sent-2411-035: We show that the eigenvalue range of Mamba and DeltaNet can be extended to $[-1,1]$ without compromising efficiency or training stability.
elements.sent-2411-036: We test the modified methods on parity, modular arithmetic, and permutation composition, demonstrating improved state-tracking performance.
elements.sent-2411-037: We pre-train modified versions of DeltaNet and Mamba (up to 1.3B parameters) and show that they reach performance comparable to the original models on generative language modeling tasks, while DeltaNet shows improved perplexity on coding and math datasets.

elements.sent-2411-062: Finite State Automata and Regular Languages.~ A (deterministic) finite state automaton (FSA) is a tuple $\mathcal{A \,{=}\, (\Sigma,Q,q_0,\delta)$ where $\Sigma$ is a finite set of letters called alphabet, $Q$ is a finite set of states, $q_0 \,{\in}\, Q$ is the starting state and $\delta\,{:}\, Q \,{\times}\, \Sigma \,{\to}\, Q$ is the state-transition function \citep[see][for an introduction]{hopcroft2001introduction .~We define the set $\Sigma^*,$ whose elements are sequences called words, as the smallest superset of $\Sigma$ that contains the empty word $\varepsilon$ and is closed under word concatenation.~We extend the state-transition function to $\delta\,{:}\, Q \,{\times}\, \Sigma^* \,{\to}\, Q$ by defining $\delta(q,\varepsilon) \,{= }\,q$ and $\delta(q, \vw) \,{=}\, \delta(\delta(q, w_1\dots w_{i-1 ), w_i)$ for any $\vw = w_1\dots w_i \in \Sigma^*$ with $i \geq 2$.~We say that $\delta(q_0, \vw)$ is the state that $\mathcal{A}$ reaches after reading the word $\vw \in \Sigma^*$.~A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is said to be recognized by $\mathcal{A}$ if there exists a recognizing set $R \,{\subseteq}\, Q$ such that $ L \,{=}\, \{\vw \,{\in}\, \Sigma^* : \delta(q_0, \vw) \,{\in}\, R\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Regular languages are the ones that can be recognized by an FSA.
elements.sent-2411-064: Given an FSA $\mathcal{A}$, the set $\mathcal{T (\mathcal{A ) \,{= }\,\{ \delta(\cdot, \vw) : \vw \in \Sigma^* \}$ of functions $\rho\,{:}\, Q \,{\to}\, Q$, together with the function composition operation forms a monoid called transition monoid, i.e. it is associative, closed and contains the identity $\delta(\cdot,\varepsilon)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: This monoid has a finite number of elements, since $|Q| \,{<}\, \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Moreover, if $\delta(\cdot, w)$ is bijective for every $w \in \Sigma$, then $\mathcal{T (\mathcal{A )$ forms a group, i.e. it contains the inverse of each element.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: State-Tracking and Monoid Word Problems.~ State-tracking is the problem of determining the state of a system only by observing a sequence of updates applied to it.
elements.sent-2411-068: Formally, it can be expressed as a monoid word problem \citep{merrillillusion , where given a monoid $(M, \cdot)$ ($M$ is the set and $\cdot$ is the associative operation), we want to send words $m_1\dots m_t \in M^*$, describing the sequence of updates, to their product $m_1 \cdot m_2 \cdots m_t \in M$, representing the state of the system after the updates.}
elements.sent-2411-069: If $M$ is finite there is a corresponding FSA $(M, M, e, \delta)$ that solves the word problem, where the starting state is $e$ (the identity element), and the transition function is $\delta(m_1, m_2) = m_2 \cdot m_1$ for $m_1,m_2\in M$.
elements.sent-2411-070: In this work, we focus on group word problems, i.e. problems where the monoid is also a group.
elements.sent-2411-071: In particular, on the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z _m$, i.e. addition modulo $m$, and the symmetric group $S_m$, i.e. the group of permutations on $m$ elements.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Parity is equivalent to the $S_2$ word problem, while many state-tracking problems such as tracking chess moves or code evaluation, can be shown to be harder than the $S_5$ word problem, which cannot be solved by Transformers and diagonal LRNNs even in log-precision for arbitrary word lengths \citep{merrillillusion,merrill2023parallelism .}

elements.sent-2411-199: In this work, we showed the substantial impact of extending the eigenvalue range of state-transition matrices in LRNNs from $[0,1]$ to $[-1,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-200: This modification provably enhances LRNN expressivity in state-tracking tasks, without adding overhead in training or inference.
elements.sent-2411-201: While Mamba successfully solves the parity problem, its diagonal matrix structure limits further performance gains.
elements.sent-2411-202: In contrast, DeltaNet, by leveraging its non-diagonal matrix structure enabling simultaneous token and channel mixing, excels across a broader spectrum of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-203: Our results underscore the critical role of non-diagonal state-transition matrices in augmenting state-tracking capabilities, highlighting a promising direction for future LRNN advancements.
elements.sent-2411-204: Limitations and Future work~ Our modification is not directly compatible with a numerical technique used by some diagonal LRNNs such as Mamba2, GLA and mLSTM.
elements.sent-2411-205: In partticular, these models rely on positive state-transition matrices to compute cumulative products in log space, which improves numerical accuracy and potentially training stability (see \Cref{app:implementation for details).}
elements.sent-2411-206: Further research is needed to assess the impact of training large-scale language models with state-tracking capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-207: To this end, we aim to understand the potential downsides of increased expressivity.
elements.sent-2411-208: For example, we hypothesize a fundamental trade-off between state-tracking and associative recall, which is also of theoretical interest and could guide hybrid model design.
elements.sent-2411-209: Moreover, the theoretical expressivity of DeltaNet $[-1,1]$ with multiple layers is still unclear.

elements.section-2411-011: LRNNs Can Do Modular Addition Using Only Reflections
elements.sent-2411-369: In this section, we explain how an LRNN with two layers and using only Householder state transition matrices (reflections) can compute addition modulo $m \in \N$, i.e it can map words $x_1, \dots, x_t$ with $x_i \in \{0,\dots,m-1\}$ into $y_t = (\sum_{i=1 ^{m x_i) \bmod m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-370: This corresponds to solving the group word problem associated with the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z _m$.}
elements.sent-2411-371: We note that our modification of DeltaNet, namely DeltaNet [-1,1] can therefore solve addition modulo $m$ with 2 layers.
elements.sent-2411-372: If the state transition matrices can be a generic rotation matrices, then a LRNN can perform addition modulo $m$ using just one layer by mapping each element of $\mathbb{Z _m$ to the corresponding $2 \times 2$ rotation matrix as shown in \Cref{proof:groups .}}
elements.sent-2411-373: Such construction requires a number of states for the LRNN equal to $m$, i.e. the number of elements of the group $\mathbb{Z _m$.}
elements.sent-2411-374: However, since we assume that state transition matrices are reflections, we cannot map each element of the group to a rotation (since those are a product of 2 reflections) and our construction for the LRNN will require two layers.
elements.sent-2411-375: Specifically, the first layer will count modulo $2$, i.e. it will output the sequence $\vy^{(1) _1, \dots, \vy^{(1) _t$ where $\vy^{(1) _i = (x_i, i \bmod 2)$, while the second layer will have $2m$ states and will use two different reflection matrices for each group element, depending on the value of $y^{(1) _{i,2 = i \bmod 2$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-376: Formally, we have the following result.
elements.sent-2411-377: In particular, the LRNN will have 2 scalar states in the first layer and $2m$ states, each being a vector in $\R^2$, in the second layer.
elements.sent-2411-378: The first layer of the LRNN will implement counting modulo $2$ as follows.. \begin{equation}h^{(1)}_{0} = 0, \quad h_t^{(1)} = - h^{(1)}_{t-1} + 1, \quad \vy^{(1)}_t = dec^{(1)}(h_t, x_t) = (x_t, h_t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-379: We note that the state-transition matrix (the scalar $-1$) is a reflection since $\{-1\} = \mathcal{M ^1_1(\{-1\})$.}
elements.sent-2411-380: For the second layer, we have instead \begin{equation}\vh^{(2)}_{0} = (1,0)^\top, \quad \vh^{(2)}_t = \mA^{(2)}(\vy^{(1)}_t) \vh^{(2)}_{t-1}, \quad \vy^{(2)}_t = dec^{(2)}(\vh_t^{(2)}, \vy^{(1)}_t) \mA^{(2)}(\vy) = \mH(\theta(y_1, y_2)) = \left[{cc} \cos \theta(y_1, y_2) \sin \theta(y_1, y_2) \sin \theta(y_1, y_2) -\cos \theta(y_1, y_2) \right] dec^{(2)}(\vh, \vy) = \argmax_{i \in \{0, \dots, m-1\}} \max (\vc_i^\top \vh, \vd_i^\top \vh) \quad\end{equation} where $\vy = (y_1, y_2)^\top \in \{0,\dots, m-1\} \times \{0,1\}$, $\mH(\alpha)$ is the $2 \times 2$ reflection matrix that reflects all vectors by a line having an angle of $\alpha/2$ with the line passing from the origin and the vector $(1,0)^\top$ and $\theta: \{0,\dots, m-1\} \times \{0,1\} \to \R$ determines the angle of the reflection and is defined as \begin{equation}\theta(i, 1) = \frac{(1 - 2i)\pi}{m}, \quad \theta(i, 0) = \frac{(2i + 1) \pi}{m}, \quad  for all  i \in \{0,\dots, m-1\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-042: \begin{equation}\vh^{(2)}_{0} = (1,0)^\top, \quad \vh^{(2)}_t = \mA^{(2)}(\vy^{(1)}_t) \vh^{(2)}_{t-1}, \quad \vy^{(2)}_t = dec^{(2)}(\vh_t^{(2)}, \vy^{(1)}_t) \mA^{(2)}(\vy) = \mH(\theta(y_1, y_2)) = \left[{cc} \cos \theta(y_1, y_2) \sin \theta(y_1, y_2) \sin \theta(y_1, y_2) -\cos \theta(y_1, y_2) \right] dec^{(2)}(\vh, \vy) = \argmax_{i \in \{0, \dots, m-1\}} \max (\vc_i^\top \vh, \vd_i^\top \vh) \quad\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-381: Moreover $\mathcal{C = \{\vc_0,\dots, \vc_{m-1}\}$ and $\mathcal{D = \{\vd_0,\dots, \vd_{m-1}\}$ are the two sets of states corresponding to reflections and rotations respectively and are defined as \begin{equation}\vd_0 = \vh_0^{(2)} = (1,0)^\top, \quad \vc_0 = \mH(\theta(0,1))\vd_0, \vd_i = \mR(2i\pi/m) \vd_0, \quad \vc_i = \mR(-2i\pi/m)\vc_0 \quad for all  i \in \{0,\dots, m-1\},\end{equation} where $\mR(\beta)$ is a rotation matrix with angle $\beta \in \R$.}}
elements.sent-2411-382: Let $\alpha,\gamma \in \R$, the following are standard identities of products of rotations and reflections. \begin{equation} \mR(\alpha) \mR(\gamma) =\mR(\alpha + \gamma), \quad \mH(\alpha)\mH(\gamma) =\mR(\alpha- \gamma), \mR(\alpha) \mH(\gamma) =\mH\left(\alpha+ \gamma\right) \mH(\gamma) \mR(\alpha) =\mH\left(\gamma-\alpha\right). \end{equation} From our choice of $\theta$, $\vd_i$ and $\vc_i$, using the identities above and the the fact that $\mR$ is a periodic function with period $2\pi$ we have that \begin{equation}\label{eq:dtoc} \begin{aligned} \mH(\theta(j,1))\vd_i = \mH(\theta(j,1))\mR(2i\pi/m)\vd_0 = \mH(\theta(j,1))\mR(2i\pi/m)\mH(\pi/m)\vc_0 = \mH(\theta(j,1))\mH(\theta(i,0))\vc_0 = \mR( \theta(j,1) - \theta(i,0))\vc_0 = \mR(-2(i+j)\pi/m)\vc_0 = \vc_{i+j \bmod m}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} and similarly \begin{equation}\label{eq:ctod} \begin{aligned} \mH(\theta(j,0))\vc_i = \mH(\theta(j,1))\mR(-2i\pi/m)\vc_0 = \mH(\theta(j,0))\mR(-2i\pi/m)\mH(\pi/m)\vd_0 = \mH(\theta(j,0))\mH(\theta(i,1))\vd_0 = \mR( \theta(j,0) - \theta(i,1))\vd_0 = \mR(2(i+j)\pi/m)\vd_0 = \vd_{i+j \bmod m}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} for every $i,j \in \{0,\dots, m-1\}$.

elements.sent-2411-425: For the full matrix baseline we use a single layer and map directly each token $x_i$ to a learnable full state-transition matrix $\mA(x_i) \in \R^{n \times n}$ via one-hot encoding.
elements.sent-2411-426: We then compute, for $i \in \{1, \dots, t\}$ the recursion \begin{equation}\mH_i = \mA(x_i)\mH_{i-1}, \quad \mH_0 = \mI \in \R^{n \times n}\end{equation} where $n$ is set to $32$ for efficiency reasons (memory and compute time grow quickly with $n$).
elements.sent-2411-427: After that, we flatten each $\mH_i$ into a vector and apply first a projection on the unit ball and then a linear decoder to get the final outputs.
elements.sent-2411-428: The projection was added to increase stability since we do not bound the norm of $\mA(x_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-429: Since this model uses a full matrix, with $n \geq 5$ it should be fully able to learn $S_5$ without restricting the transitions in input or using more tokens per transition.
elements.sent-2411-430: However, in some situations, the performance degrades quickly after some input sequence length, probably because the norm of the learned $\mA(x_i)$ is not close enough to one.

elements.sent-2411-614: Linear Recurrent Neural Networks (LRNNs) such as Mamba, RWKV, GLA, mLSTM, and DeltaNet have emerged as efficient alternatives to Transformers for long sequences.
elements.sent-2411-615: However, both Transformers and LRNNs struggle to perform state-tracking, which may impair performance in tasks such as code evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-616: In one forward pass, current architectures are unable to solve even parity, the simplest state-tracking task, which non-linear RNNs can handle effectively.
elements.sent-2411-617: We extend this result to non-diagonal LRNNs such as DeltaNet.
elements.sent-2411-618: We prove that finite precision LRNNs with state-transition matrices having only positive eigenvalues cannot solve parity, while non-triangular matrices are needed to count modulo $3$.
elements.sent-2411-619: Our experiments confirm that extending the eigenvalue range of Mamba and DeltaNet to include negative values not only enables them to solve parity but consistently improves their performance on state-tracking tasks.
elements.sent-2411-620: We also show that state-tracking enabled LRNNs can be pretrained stably and efficiently at scale (1.3B parameters), achieving competitive performance on language modeling and showing promise on code and math tasks.
elements.sent-2411-621: Surprisingly, both Transformers and current LRNNs face a fundamental limitation: they struggle to learn how to track the state of even simple finite-state machines from sequences of state-transitions \citep{deletangneural .}
elements.sent-2411-622: This limitation may impair performance on tasks such as entity tracking in narratives, handling nested structures in code, and reasoning tasks that can benefit from maintaining and updating an internal state over time~\citep{merrillillusion .}
